coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photovoltaic performance of inverted polymer solar cells utilizing versatile chemically functionalized ZnO@graphene quantum dot monolayer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Moon, Byung Joon
Lee, Kyu Seung
Shim, Jaeho
Park, Soohyung
Kim, Se Ho
Bae, Sukang
Park, Min
Lee, Chang-Lyoul
Choi, Won Kook
Yi, Yeonjin
Hwang, Jun Yeon
Son, Dong Ick
coredata.dc:description: Recently, interfacial engineering approaches as an efficient strategy for improving the power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs) has attracted considerable attention. Among various efficient solutions, solution-processed metal-oxide films prepared from metal oxide sol–gel precursors (or nanoparticles) and polymer surface modifiers are typically used as electron selective interfaces in the inverted cell geometry. To present a more effective strategy for surpassing the limitations of traditional methods, such as an unintended increase in series or contact resistance by incompatibility at the organic/inorganic interface, inherently insulating nature of non-conjugated surface modifiers and oxygen adsorption (or photo-induced doping) of metal-oxide layer, we synthesize chemically surface-modified ZnO@graphene core–shell type quantum dots (ZGQDs) with well-characterization of the chemical, optical and electrical properties, and fabricate iPSCs consisting of ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/photoactive layer/MoO3/Ag. The mono-layered QDs play the multi-functional role as surface modifier, sub-photosensitizer and electron transport layer. Using this effective approach, we achieve the highest conversion efficiency of ~10.3% resulting from improved interfacial properties and efficient charge transfer based on static quenching and charge transfer reaction from ZnO to graphene nanosheets (with drastically reduced τavg (~ 60ps)), which is verified by various analysis tools.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photovoltaic
Inverted polymer solar cells
Functionalized
ZnO@graphene
Quantum dot
Monolayer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnO@Graphene core-shell quantum dots (ZGQDs)
Synthesis of octylamine functionalized ZnO@Graphene core–shell quantum dots (ZGQD-OAs)
Structural and optical characterization of ZnO based quantum dots (ZnO QDs, ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs)
Fabrication and characterization of inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical modification (functionalization) process and characterization. Schematic illustration of chemical modification from ZnO@Graphene quantum dots (ZGQDs) to octylamine functionalized ZnO@Graphene quantum dots (ZGQD-OAs) (a). Surface functionalization through chemical reactions of graphene nanoshell (b). XPS C 1s core-level spectra of ZGQDs (inset) and ZGQD-OAs (c) films on Si/SiO2/Au (50nm) substrate. FT-IR spectra of ZGQDs (black line) and ZGQD-OAs (red line) (d).

Optical characterization and Modelling of DOS. Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO (black), ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs dispersed in ethanol (a). PL spectra (λex: 360nm) for ZnO (black line), ZGQDs (red line) and ZGQD-OAs (blue line) dispersed in ethanol (inset: Normalized PL spectra for ZnO, ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs) (b). Optimized structures of “Pristine graphene” (top), “Epoxylated graphene” (left) and “Amidated graphene” (right) (c). Calculated density of states (DOS) of “Amidated graphene” (blue), “Epoxylated graphene” (red) and “Pristine graphene” (black). MO energy is marked by vertical bars in each calculated DOS (d). PL transition scheme for ZGQDs (left) and ZGQD-OAs (right) (e).

Devices fabrication process and characterization. Device structure of the inverted PSCs with/without the ZGQD-OAs monolayer (a), (b). Cross-sectional TEM image of the inverted PSCs with a ZGQD-OAs monolayer and magnified image of ZGQD-OAs/PEIE/ITO layer (inset) (c). Chemical structure of PEIE. The magnified image of electrostatic interaction between the functional groups of PEIE and ZGQD-OAs (d). Schematic illustration of the structures of PEIE/PTB7:PC71BM and PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM by surface treatment (e).

Characterization of iPSC devices. Energy level diagram of the inverted PSCs highlighting pathways for charge generation (a) and transport (b). TRPL spectra of ITO/PEIE/PTB7:PC71BM (red line) and ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM (black line) film (λex: 420nm) (c). J-V characteristics of the inverted PSCs with/without the ZGQD-OAs monolayer under the illumination of AM 1.5, 100mWcm−2 (d). EQE spectra of the inverted PSCs with/without the ZGQD-OAs monolayer (e).

UPS characterization of devices. UPS spectra for bare ITO (black line), ITO/PEIE (red line) and ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs (blue line) samples (a). Plausible energy level diagram changes triggered by the shifted conduction band energy of ITO electrode under open circuit conditions (b). Plausible energy level diagram changes triggered by the shifted conduction band energy of ITO electrode under short circuit (c).

head.ce:title: Enhanced photovoltaic performance of inverted polymer solar cells utilizing versatile chemically functionalized ZnO@graphene quantum dot monolayer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, interfacial engineering approaches as an efficient strategy for improving the power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs) has attracted considerable attention. Among various efficient solutions, solution-processed metal-oxide films prepared from metal oxide sol–gel precursors (or nanoparticles) and polymer surface modifiers are typically used as electron selective interfaces in the inverted cell geometry. To present a more effective strategy for surpassing the limitations of traditional methods, such as an unintended increase in series or contact resistance by incompatibility at the organic/inorganic interface, inherently insulating nature of non-conjugated surface modifiers and oxygen adsorption (or photo-induced doping) of metal-oxide layer, we synthesize chemically surface-modified ZnO@graphene core–shell type quantum dots (ZGQDs) with well-characterization of the chemical, optical and electrical properties, and fabricate iPSCs consisting of ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/photoactive layer/MoO3/Ag. The mono-layered QDs play the multi-functional role as surface modifier, sub-photosensitizer and electron transport layer. Using this effective approach, we achieve the highest conversion efficiency of ~10.3% resulting from improved interfacial properties and efficient charge transfer based on static quenching and charge transfer reaction from ZnO to graphene nanosheets (with drastically reduced τavg (~ 60ps)), which is verified by various analysis tools.

ce:section.ce:para: Bulk heterojunction (BHJ) polymer solar cells (PSCs) based on composites of conjugated polymers and fullerene derivatives have attracted attention due to their potential as renewable, lightweight and low-cost energy sources [1–5]. The main challenges for PSCs are the achievement of long-term air stability and comparative power conversion efficiencies (PCEs). Generally, BHJ PSCs are fabricated with a low work function metal cathode, such as aluminum (Al) and calcium (Ca), a transparent conductive anode, such as indium tin oxide (ITO) and fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO), and a photoactive layer deposited between the cathode and anode [6–8]. However, devices with such structures undergo degradation in ambient conditions. There are reports which confirmed that, the diffusion of moisture and oxygen molecules into PSCs has fatally damaged the low work function (WF) metal cathode [9–11]. Also, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), which is widely known as an anode buffer layer, has negative effects on active layer and ITO electrode due to its acidic and hygroscopic properties [12–14].

Therefore, to improve the device stability, the inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs) have been developed using metal oxides (i.e. titanium oxide (TiOX), cesium carbonate (CS2CO3) and zinc oxide (ZnO)) as electron transport layer (ETL) [15–21]. Among the n-type metal oxides used in iPSCs, ZnO is a prospective candidate due to its relatively high electron mobility, good long-term stability and high transparency in the visible light region [22–24]. Despite many advantages, however, the performance of iPSCs is somewhat limited due to inherent incompatibility between the organic active layer and the inorganic ZnO layer which leads to charge recombination and high contact resistance [25,26]. Alternatively, in order to not only improve compatibility at organic/inorganic layer interface but also electron collection efficiency, some researchers have reported improvement of device performance using fullerene doped ZnO nanometer-thick film on ITO electrode [27]. However, the reason behind efficiency enhancement still not clear and they also have not fully characterized the changes in the opto-electronic behaviors of the ZnO based QDs.

Recently, non-conjugated viscous polymers (i.e. poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI) and poly-(ethyleneimine)-ethoxylated (PEIE)), which are low-cost basic polymers dissolved in alcohol based solvents, have been introduced to decrease the WF of ITO due to the formation of dipole layer at the ITO/PEI (or PEIE) interfaces [28,29]. It is further reported that, the multi-functionality of QDs monolayer, through interface control as interlayer on the PEIE layer, showed good electron selectivity for organic devices [30]. Accordingly, there is a need to develop low-defect, low-contact resistances, and uniform interface between inorganic metal oxide and organic layer so as to realize high-efficiency inverted polymer solar cells.

In this paper, we report novel approach to enhance the photovoltaic performance by the advantages of chemically functionalized ZnO@graphene core–shell type quantum dots (ZGQDs) with octylamine. The mono-layered ZGQDs play the multi-functional role as surface modifier, photosensitizer and electron transport layer. Also, we demonstrate the synthesis and characterization of chemically surface-modified ZGQDs involving the chemical, optical and electrical properties. The fundamental understanding of photovoltaic behaviors for the fabricated iPSCs structures with the mono-layered ZGQDs is described on the basis of the performance such as characterization of chemically surface-modified QDs, modeling of density functional theory (DFT), ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), and time-resolved PL measurements.

Graphite powder (Alfa Aesar, 3g) was ultrasonicated with 72ml of a 3:1 mixture of sulfuric acid (H2SO4, Sigma Aldrich, 95.0–98.0%) and nitric acid (HNO3, Sigma Aldrich, ≥90%) for 120min, while maintaining the temperature at 50°C. The solution was maintained at a room temperature for 5 days until the color of the dispersion changed to charcoal. And then, the dispersion was rinsed repeatedly with DI-water and ethanol in a cycle of centrifugation and decantation. Lastly, the final greyish product was allowed to dry at 60°C for 24h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO@Graphene core-shell quantum dots (ZGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: GO (30mg) was well-dispersed in 30ml of N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, HCON(CH3)2, Sigma Aldrich, anhydrous, 99.8%) by ultrasonication for 30min. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99.999% 0.69g) was dissolved in 200ml of DMF, and then the GO solution was added while continually stirring to form a stable precursor. Then, the mixed solution was heated to 110°C and maintained at that temperature for 4h. The color of the ZGQD then changed to white-greyish. Subsequently, the solution was washed 4 times with ethanol by centrifugation, and finally with DI-water. Lastly, the final product (ZGQD powder) was obtained after drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of octylamine functionalized ZnO@Graphene core–shell quantum dots (ZGQD-OAs)
ce:section.ce:para: 100mL of octylamine solution (0.15M, in ethanol) was gradually injected into 500mL of ZGQD solution (5mg/mL) and stirring at 80°C for 24h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was further centrifuged (Solvents: Hexane, Ethanol and IPA). Finally, the final product was collected after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of ZnO based quantum dots (ZnO QDs, ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs)
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Jasco V-670 and Holiba NanoLog-C. The Raman spectra were collected using a Horiba Jobin Yvon-Labram HR UV–Visible–NIR Raman microscope spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, JASCO FT/IR-6600) are employed for the QDs contain relative amount of nitrogen and oxygen groups. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-alpha) was performed using monochromated Al Kα X-ray photons (hν=1486.6eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs)
ce:section.ce:para: Solar cell devices were fabricated on 1.5×1.5cm2 pre-patterned ITO coated glass substrates. The ITO-coated glass substrates were cleaned with ethanol, acetone and 2-propanol. The ITO-coated glass was treated with O2 plasma for 40sec. PEIE solution was spin-coated with 6nm thickness and the PEIE film was baked at 110°C for 10min under ambient conditions. After cooling, the OA-ZG interfacial layer was spin-coated on the surface of the PEIE layer at 1000rpm for 40sec, followed by thermal annealing at 110°C for 25min. The substrates were then transferred into a nitrogen-filled glove box. The blend of PTB7:PC71BM (or PTB7-Th:PC71BM, 1:1.5 (by weight)) solution were spin-coated onto the PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer to form a 120nm thick active layer. Finally, MoO3 (10nm) and Ag (80nm) were sequentially deposited on the active layer by thermal evaporation in a vacuum of 5×10−7 Torr, which defines the active area of 0.0464cm2. The un-encapsulated solar cells were tested under ambient conditions using a Keithley 2400 SMU and an Oriel xenon lamp (450W) with an AM 1.5G filter. A mask was used to define the device illumination area of 0.0464cm2 to minimize photocurrent generation from the edge of the electrodes. The solar cells (with no protective encapsulation) were then tested in air under an AM 1.5G illumination of 100mWcm−2 (Oriel 1kW solar simulator), which was calibrated with the International System of Units (SI) (SRC-1000-TC-KG5-N, VLSI Standards, Inc) for accurate measurement. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured using an Oriel IQE-200 (NewPort), a calibrated Si UV detector, and an SR8570 low noise current amplifier. In order to confirm the surface morphology, AFM imaging was carried out in air using an NX-10 (Park systems). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20), at the acceleration voltage 200kV, was carried out to investigate the cross-sectional structure of the inverted PSCs using focused ion beam (FIB, Helios NanoLab 650) milling techniques. Static contact angles were determined using a contact angle and surface tension analyzer (Phoenix 300-Touch, Seo Co). Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS measurement was performed using He I (21.2eV) discharge lamp. The photoluminescence lifetimes were carefully measured in TRPL experiments by excitation with ~150fs pulses derived from a 76MHz amplified Ti:sapphire laser with a wavelength of 750nm.

ZGQDs, which were synthesized by chemical method, are n-type hybrid quantum dots consisting of a ZnO core covered with graphene nanoshells [33,34]. However, ZGQDs have low dispersibility in non-polar organic solvents, such as toluene and o-xylene, due to the presence of relatively large amounts of oxygen-containing groups and also lack of long alkyl chains that can help enhance the solubility in various aromatic solvents. As shown in Figure S1, ZGQDs are well-dispersed in ethanol (relatively polar solvent) up to 12h whereas they are quickly precipitated in less than a minute in toluene (relatively non-polar solvent). However, PEIE layer deposited onto ITO electrode can be easily damaged by polar organic solvents such as ethanol, IPA and DI-water. Therefore, surface functionalization of ZGQDs with octylamine (OA) is necessary to make well-disperse solution in relatively non-polar organic solvents. In our experiments, the alkylamine functionalization occurred through two chemical reactions of graphene nanoshell ( Fig. 1a). In one reaction, the amine groups react with epoxy groups on the surface of the graphene nanoshell via ring-opening amination of epoxides to yield 1,2-amino alcohols [31,32]. In another reaction, the amines interact with carboxylic groups along the edge of the graphene nanoshell to form either amides or alkylammonium ions [35,36]. After functionalization of ZGQDs, as shown in Figure S1, ZGQD-OAs are well-dispersed in both ethanol and toluene for over 10min.

In order to confirm the successful completion of the chemical reaction, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were performed. XPS was performed to characterize the elemental composition and the carbon bonding configuration of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs. Fig. 1c shows core level XPS spectra of C 1s and N 1s for the ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum (inset of Fig. 1c) for ZGQDs shows signals corresponding to sp2 C–C and π–π* satellite peak at 284.5 and 290.6eV, respectively, along with the peaks related to O-rich groups such as C–O (or C–O–C), C=O, and O–C=O bond at 286.6, 288.3, and 289.0eV, respectively [37]. In contrast, the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum for ZGQD-OAs shows the absence of O–C=O peak and with decreased intensity for peak related to epoxy groups at 286.6eV from 6.04% to 4.35%.

In addition, a new peak related to C–N bond at 285.2eV appears in the C 1s XPS spectrum for ZGQD-OAs, associated with corresponding peak in their N 1s XPS spectrum at a binding energy of about 400eV (Fig. 1c and Figure S2), indicating that nucleophilic addition of alkylamine with graphene nanoshell has been conducted successfully [38]. The FT-IR spectra of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs are shown in Fig. 1d. After the functionalization of ZGQDs with octylamine, there is drastic change in the intensities of the peaks corresponding to the oxygen related functional groups, such as the –COOH vibration peak at 3000–3650cm−1 (broad) and ~1730cm−1, C–O (or C–O–C) vibration peak at ~1250 and ~1050cm−1, and –OH vibration peak at ~3430cm−1 [38]. On the other hand, two prominent new peaks in the region between ~2830 and ~2970cm−1 and a single sharp peak at ~1390cm−1 appeared, which are related to the C–H stretching and bending vibrations in the –CH2 of the alkylamines, respectively [39]. Moreover, the peak due to the amide bond (–CONH) at ~1650cm−1 is observed [40]. These findings confirm not only the removal of oxygen-containing groups from the surface of graphene nanoshells but also the formation of covalent bond of alkylamine with graphene surface.

To further characterize the structural changes of graphene nanoshells during the functionalization process, we obtained Raman spectra of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs as shown in Figure S3. Raman spectroscopy is highly sensitive to the electronic structure and is an essential tool for the characterization of carbon based materials [41,42]. Raman spectroscopy of graphene nanoshells is generally characterized by two main peaks at around 1351 and 1579cm−1 corresponding to the well-defined D and G band, which come from the first order scattering of the E2g phonon of the sp2 carbon atoms and from the breathing mode of κ-point phonons with A1g symmetry, respectively [42]. After functionalization, the D and G band of graphene nanoshells are not significantly changed. However, it is noteworthy that the intensity ratio of D band to G band (I
D
/I
G ) of graphene nanoshells substantially increases after the chemical reaction with octylamine. This observation indicates that covalent modification of ZGQDs is completed successfully, resulting in an increase in disorder due to the formation of more sp3 carbons on the graphene nanoshells.

To understand the modifications in the optical properties after the functionalization reaction of ZGQDs, firstly, we obtained UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of QDs as shown in Fig. 2a. Compared to pristine ZnO QDs, the absorption peak maximum of ZGQDs (or ZGQD-OAs), originating from the existence of good crystalline and impurity-suppressed ZnO nanostructures, is red-shifted, which is ascribed to the conjugation of graphene [34] and graphene-OA to the surface of ZnO QDs.

Interestingly, the presence of graphene triggers increased light absorption at longer wavelengths due to the surface electric charge effect and modification of the exciton formation process during UV–vis irradiation. Furthermore, the additional enhancement of absorption intensity in the longer wavelengths (400–800nm) could be rationalized on the basis of the accompanied surface modification during amidation reaction of the O-rich groups leading to the reduction of graphene nanoshells, which is in good accordance with previous reports [43]. Further, in order to precisely understand the enhanced absorption at longer wavelength (around 400nm), we obtained the PL spectra of QDs at room temperature. The PL spectrum of ZnO QDs exhibits only a near-band-edge excitonic emission as shown Fig. 2b [44]. On the other hand, the PL spectra of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs are substantially quenched, caused by an efficient charge separation by direct electron transfer from ZnO QDs to the graphene through a chemical bonding of Zn–O–C [33]. Also, inset in Fig. 2b illustrating the enlarged PL spectra of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs shows that new PL peaks around 400nm (ca. 3.1eV) appear. Interestingly, PL spectrum of ZGQD-OAs in the range from 400 to 440nm is significantly changed compared with that of ZGQDs. The conjugation of graphene-OA to the ZnO QDs leads to a UV PL emission quenching to a ratio of about 64.7% along with the appearance of new graphene-OA related peaks. The narrow UV peak around 387nm, generally appears for ZnO QDs, is clearly attributed to the interband emission from ZnO QD. Therefore, it can be assumed that the alkylamine functionalization has an effect on exciton generation and electron transition mechanism.

To understand the changes in PL spectra, the density of states (DOS) was estimated with simple model using density functional theory (DFT). We modeled local structure of graphene with 19 aromatic rings with hydrogen termination at the edge for modeling the outer graphene shell. This model has described graphene reasonably [45]. We modeled ZGQDs with epoxy structures as reported and modeled the ZGQD-OAs by attaching amide(C8) and hydroxyl group to the graphene shell since it is expected that the octylamine would interact with graphene as shown in Fig. 1a [33]. A Becke-style three-parameter Lee-Yang-Parr hybrid functional with a split basis set of 6-31G* was adopted with Gaussian package software. We relaxed all possible structures of ZGQD-OAs shown in Fig. 1a to find out an energetic minimum. ZGQDs model shows good agreement with PL spectra and previous results as expected [33]. Then, we attached the amide and hydroxyl group to the graphene (Fig. 2c Amidated graphene, ZGQD-OAs) and found an energy minimum structure. Fig. 2d shows the calculated DOS of ZGQD-OAs, ZGQDs and pristine graphene, which reveals unique energy levels near the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). In Fig. 2d, only p orbitals contribute to the LUMO level of pristine graphene whereas ZGQD-OAs and ZGQDs spectra show that LUMO level is split into three levels (LUMO, LUMO+1 and LUMO+2) and the ratio of s and p orbital contribution is changed. These epoxy and amide groups break the symmetry of the C–C bonds in graphene and induce the s-orbital contribution by the orbital hybridization [33]. The DOS of epoxylated graphene (ZGQDs) has the LUMO and LUMO+2 levels with 7.1% and 6.7% s orbital contribution, while the amidated graphene (ZGQD-OAs) has 11.4% s orbital contribution only at LUMO level (Table S1). This spectrum change implies that amide group strongly affects the orbital hybridization of ZGQDs. The PL spectra of ZGQDs and ZGQD-OAs are caused by the electron transfer from ZnO QDs to the graphene through a chemical bonding of Zn–O–C. Thus, photo-excited electrons from the O 2p of the ZnO quantum dots are transferred to the LUMO level of epoxylated and amidated graphene (See Fig. 2e). Since this transition is governed by the selection rule (∆l=± 1), only electrons in s or d orbital (l=0 or 2) are allowed to transit from graphene shell to the O 2p (l=1) valence level for the ZnO QDs. ZGQDs have two s orbital states (Fig. 2c Mark B and B׳, 2.37 and 1.75eV) at LUMO and LUMO+2, which contribute to the PL process in addition to the ZnO QDs main emission. In Fig. 2b, the 404 (3.06eV) and the 433nm (2.86eV) of two additional PL peaks are observed. This is good agreement with calculated DOS and the previous results [34]. In the ZGQD-OAs case, only one additional PL emission (Fig. 2c Mark A, 2.23eV) is observed beside the ZnO QDs main peak. This agrees very well with the DFT results.

In order to verify the effect of ZnO based QDs on device performance, we fabricated inverted architecture BHJ PSCs with the active blend layer of PTB7 (or PTB7-Th) and PC71BM as shown in Figs. 3a and 3b. However, as aforementioned, ZGQDs are hardly soluble in aromatic non-polar solvents, such as toluene and chlorobenzene, we could only use ZGQD-OAs as cathode interfacial layer.

Before trying to understand the reasons for enhanced PCEs using a ZGQD-OAs layer in PSCs, we obtained surface images of ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs using high‐resolution cross‐sectional transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to confirm whether the ZGQD-OAs layer were actually coated on the ITO/PEIE surface. Fig. 3c shows cross-sectional TEM images of multi-layered inverted PSCs with PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer. A ZGQD-OAs monolayer in the multilayered structures was examined through TEM using site-specific TEM specimen preparation using focused ion beam (FIB) milling technique. The surface coverage of QDs monolayer on PEIE/ITO/glass was precisely estimated to be monolayer from the image analysis of the cross-sectional TEM picture showing packed QDs monolayer in the insect of Fig. 3c. It can be seen that there is an apparent separation between the multilayers (PTB7:PC71BM, ZGQD-OAs, PEIE and ITO) and formation of the ZGQD-OAs monolayer caused by electrostatic interaction between the positively charged protonated –NH (or –NR) groups of the PEIE and the negatively charged deprotonated -OH groups of the graphene nanoshells, or between the negatively charged deprotonated –OH groups of the PEIE and the positively charged amide (–NH) groups of the graphene nanoshells [29]. Furthermore, long alkyl chains attached to the graphene can effectively hinder the vertical stacking of ZGQD-OAs by disturbing the intermolecular π–π interactions among graphene nanoshells. In AFM topography image, the surface morphology looks little different between the ITO electrode surface and the PEIE coated ITO surface, whereas we can observe significant changes in the surface morphology before and after coating the ZGQD-OAs layer on the ITO/PEIE layer (Figure S4). It is noteworthy that the surface of PEIE/ZGQD-OAs layer (root mean square roughness (RMS): 2.282nm) is much smoother than that of intrinsic PEIE layer (RMS: 3.266nm). Based on previous reports, the reduction of the surface roughness caused by various surface treatments can effectively decrease both the charge transport distance and contact resistance [46]. Therefore, in this case, improved physical contact between two layers leads to enhancements of the J SC (15.6mA/cm2→16.2mA/cm2) and FF (0.66→0.73), as supported by the decreased series resistance (R S, 5.0 Ω cm2→3.1 Ω cm2) and increased shunt resistance (R Sh, 1286.3 Ω cm2→1554.2 Ω cm2). To obtain furthermore evidence of improved physical contact with the active layer, we calculated the surface energies of intrinsic PEIE layer and PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer by measuring the glycerol (ΘGC) and diiodomethane contact angles (ΘDIM) as shown in Figure S5. After coating with the ZGQD-OAs layer on the surface of PEIE layer, the surface energy could be reduced from 56.9 mN m−1 to 44.5 mN m−1, which is much closer to that of PC71BM (36.4 mN m−1) and PTB7 (25.1 mN m−1) [46]. In our previous reports, we demonstrated that controlling surface energies can improve the interface compatibility between two different materials [47,48]. In this regard, we can expect that ZGQD-OAs coated PEIE layer will have a favorable influence upon the interface with active layer, leading to enhancements of the photovoltaic performance (Fig. 3d). From the above studies, we schematized the significant changes observed in the surface morphology before and after coating the ZGQD-OAs layer on the ITO/PEIE layer as shown in Fig. 3e.

Fig. 4d shows the J-V curves of the iPSCs fabricated from PTB7:PC71BM blend solutions with/without the ZGQD-OAs monolayer. A significant improvement of the photovoltaic performance based on the ZGQD-OAs monolayer can be observed (Fig. 4d, Table S4). The introduction of ZGQD-OAs layer between PEIE and PTB7:PC71BM led to a 17.8% enhancement in avg. PCE (7.3%→8.6%). The champion cell shows a PCE of 8.9% and the corresponding relative enhancement is 18.7%. It suggests that the PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer plays a critical role in charge generation and transport processes between ITO and active layer (PTB7:PC71BM). Also, we examined the stability of the randomly selected iPSCs with/without the ZGQD-OAs interfacial layer over 100hours for lifetime of devices with no-encapsulation in air under ambient conditions (Figure S7). Additionally, to determine general suitability of the PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer and obtain higher power conversion efficiency, we fabricated BHJ iPSCs using PTB7-Th as an electron donor, instead of PTB7. In Fig. 4d, control device consisting of ITO/PEIE/PTB7-Th:PC71BM/MoO3/Ag yielded an average PCE of 8.8%, whereas the PTB7-Th:PC71BM BHJ iPSCs with a combined layer of PEIE and ZGQD-OAs exhibited a higher avg. PCE of 10.0% (Max. PCE: 10.3%), similar to those of the PTB7:PC71BM system. As a result, these performances indicate that our PEIE/ZGQD-OAs double layer is a promising candidate for use in high efficiency iPSCs or other photovoltaic devices.

To identify reasons for enhancing the photovoltaic performance of iPSCs, we obtained the external quantum efficiency (EQE) curves shown in Fig. 4e for devices with and without ZGQD-OAs layer. As expected, direct photoexcitation of ZGQD-OAs adds to the photocurrent in the wavelength range of the UV region (around 380nm). The EQE in the wavelength range of 350<λ<400nm exhibits a peak enhancement from 40% to 60% upon introduction of ZGQD-OAs with the ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/PTB7-Th:PC71BM/MoO3/Ag structures. Accordingly, the iPSCs of the ZGQD-OAs monolayer between PEIE and PTB7-Th:PC71BM led to a 50% enhancement in EQE. In detail, in Figure S6, the PEIE/PTB7 layer shows strong absorption from 500 to 800nm whereas the absorption from 300 to 500nm is relatively weak. As such, the addition of the ZGQD-OAs interfacial layer to PTB7 based iPSCs should in theory improve device spectral response. Indeed, as shown in Fig. 4d, devices with the ZGQD-OAs monolayer show the additional spectral responses between 300 and 600nm. Based on our previous reports, the PL quenching of ZnO QDs (see. Fig. 2b) in the presence of graphene nanoshells is mainly originated from static quenching and charge-transfer reactions [33,34]. The static quenching of ZGQD-OAs is triggered by the help of strong coupling between ZnO and graphene nanoshells (Zn–O–C bonding). In general, this quenching process occurs when both the ZnO (donor) and graphene nanoshells (acceptor) are in the ground state (ground-state complex formation). Another possible quenching mechanism is caused by the fast deactivation of the excited state by the electron transfer reactions from the conduction band of the ZnO to the LUMO of the graphene nanoshells with high electron mobility (charge transfer reaction). Consequently, under illumination, most excited electrons in the conduction band of ZnO are efficiently transferred to LUMOs of the graphene nanoshells via a charge hopping mechanism, and finally are well-collected at the ITO electrode through a PEIE layer (Fig. 4a). Also, as shown in Fig. 4b, the photo-generated electrons in the active layer (PTB7:PC71BM) can be easily transported to ITO electrode through high conductive graphene nanoshells. To understand the enhanced charge transport performance between the ZGQD-OAs and the PTB7:PC71BM heterojunction with respect to the excited electron–hole recombination, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4c, PL lifetimes of the ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM structure decrease significantly in comparison with those of the reference of PTB7:PC71BM structure. The calculated average lifetimes at 750nm for the PTB7:PC71BM and ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM structures are 220ps and 60ps, respectively (Table S3). Also, from time-resolved PL measurements, we confirmed that the charge transport between PC71BM of PTB7:PC71BM heterojunction layer and ZGQD-OAs layer in the iPSC devices is through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Since the PTB7:PC71BM heterojunction layer is closely located to the surface of ZGQD-OAs monolayer in the iPSC devices, the charge transfer from PCBM molecules of PTB7:PC71BM heterojunction to the ZGQD-OAs is attributed to the FRET process. The FRET process becomes dominant when the donor-to-acceptor distance is smaller than 10nm [49]. The TEM image shown in Fig. 3c indicates that the distance from the PTB7:PC71BM heterojunction layer to the ZGQD-OAs monolayer surface is certainly smaller than 10nm. Thus, the FRET efficiency of the charge transfer can be estimated using the following equation [49]:
(1)
η = 1 −
(
τ
D A
/
τ
D
)
,
where τDA and τD are the fluorescence lifetimes of a donor (PTB7:PC71BM) in the presence and in the absence of an acceptor (ZGQD-OAs), respectively. Table S3 shows the as-estimated FRET efficiency for the structure with PL lifetimes and the FRET efficiency, which is determined from the time-resolved PL data (73.4% at 750nm). The electron decay of the ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM structure at selected wavelength (750nm) was much faster than that for the reference sample of PTB7:PC71BM structure, which indicates the existence of an additional high-efficiency relaxation channel and confirms the efficient electron transfer from the PC71BM to the ZGQD-OAs in this case.

These observations indicate that inserting ZGQD-OAs monolayer can (1) create an additional interfacial area for exciton dissociation that might improve the charge transfer rate and (2) trigger the photo-induced charge transfer resulting from static quenching and charge-transfer reactions. Additionally, it might (3) act as a hole-blocking site caused by deep HOMO energy levels of ZnO while electrons are easily transferred from the PC71BM (LUMO energy level: 4.0eV) to ZnO (LUMO energy level: 4.1eV) or graphene nanosheets (WF: 4.3eV).

Furthermore, we carried out and analyzed the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements of both ITO/PEIE and ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs structures. The bare ITO exhibits the WF of 4.8eV as calculated from the secondary electron cutoff in Fig. 5a. After a thin PEIE layer (ca. 5nm) was coated on ITO, its WF decreased down to 3.94eV. Although this WF decrease is not enough for high performance PSCs, it is also not possible to increase PEIE thickness due to its insulating nature (band gap of ~6eV) [28]. Too thick PEIE affects on the performance of PSCs adversely as shown in Figure S8. Interestingly, without increasing PEIE thickness, the WF of the ITO is reduced further with the ZGQD-OAs monolayer on the ITO/PEIE from 3.94eV to 3.72eV (Fig. 5b). The increased built-in voltage (V BI) due to the increased WF contributes to (1) the improved V OC (from 0.73V to 0.75V, Fig. 5c), also, (2) larger band bending at the PC71BM surface under short circuit conditions (Fig. 5c, more detailed in Table S2), and thus the electrons can be effectively transported to the ITO electrode, resulting in the enhanced J SC (from 15.6 to 16.2mA/cm2) and FF (from 0.66 to 0.73).

In summary, we have synthesized organic solvent-soluble alkylamine functionalized ZGQDs under classical amidation and esterification reaction conditions, supported by spectroscopic studies through FT-IR, Raman and XPS measurements. Also, we investigated the changes in the optical properties of the ZnO based QDs through UV–vis absorption, PL, and TRPL measurements, which are in good agreement with calculated DOS of both epoxylated and amidated graphene. Finally, to confirm the effect of ZGQD-OAs monolayer on device performance, we fabricated iPSCs with the structure ITO/PEIE/ZGQD-OAs/PTB7:PC71BM/MoO3/Ag. This monolayer plays multi-functional role as photo-sensitive layer, electron transport layer and surface modifier for improving photovoltaic performance. Through several optimization process, we obtained the highest efficiency of the iPSCs with the ZGQD-OAs coated PEIE layers (champion cell) has been achieved up to 8.9% (PTB7:PC71BM) and 10.3% (PTB7-Th:PC71BM). We believe that our efficient approach can present opportunities for enhancing the performance in PSCs or other photovoltaic devices in the near future with further study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author, Dong Ick Son and Byung Joon Moon, would like to appreciate for the financial support from KIST Institution Program. The author, Won Kook Choi, would like to appreciate for the partial support from KIST research program (2E25430).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2015.11.039.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Byung Joon Moon received the B.S degree in Chemical Engineering from Sungkyunkwan Univeristy (SKKU) in 2011 and the M.S. degree from Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Korea, in 2013. Then, he has worked as a research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). His primary research interests include nano-material synthesis (0-2D) and its opto-electronic applications.

Kyu seung Lee received his B.S. Degree in Advanced material engineering from Jeonju University in 2014. Then entered Flexible and Printable Electronics from the Chonbuk National University in 2014 for M.S. course. He is also a researcher in the Korea Institude of Science and Technology (KIST). His interests include nano-material synthesis (0-2D) and its opto-electronic applications including OPV, LED devices.

Jaeho Shim received his M.S degree in electrical engineering from Hanyang University. He is currently a Ph.D. candidate at Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST). He is also a researcher in the Korea Institude of Science and Technology (KIST). His research interests include nano-material synthesis (0-2D) and its optoelectronic applications, memory devices and nanophotonics.

Soohyung Park received a Master׳s degree in Physics from the University of Yonsei in 2013. Later in the same year he began a Ph.D at the University of Yonsei. His recent research interest is focused on the electronic structures of molecular and polymer materials and related devices.

Se Ho Kim received the master’s degree from Jeon-ju University, Korea, in 2015. He currently joined Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) as a research scientist in 2015. His research focuse on surface structure analysis of 2D materials.

Sukang Bae received his MS/PhD degree in Sungkyunkwan Advanced Institute Nanoscience and Technology (SAINT) at Sungkyunkwan University, in 2012. Since 2013, he has worked as a senior research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). His primary research focus is in nano-material synthesis (0~2D), fabrication for nano-composite and related functional applications, especially for electronic and opto-electronics.

Min Park received the Ph.D. degree from the Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea, in 1994. He joined the University of Michigan Ann Arbor as a Post Doctoral Researcher in 1996. He has been working for Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) for more than 27 years is now working as a principal researcher of Photo-electronic Hybrid Research Center. His research interests include 2D nanomaterials for soft electronics, microelectronic packaging materials and carbon based polymer composites.

Chang-Lyoul Lee received his Ph.D. degree in Materials Science and Engineering from the Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology, Korea, in 2003. From 2005 to 2007, he worked as a visiting researcher in Cavendish Laboratory, University of Cambridge. Since 2007, he has worked as a senior and principle researcher in Advanced Photonics Research Institute, Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology. His research interests include optical spectroscopy and optical characterization of organic and conjugated polymer, opto-electronic devices (OLED, OPV) and sensors. He has published more than 75 articles.

Won Kook Choi received the ph.D degree in physics from Yonsei University Korea, in 1993. After that, he joined University of Oregon, USA and then Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) as a post-doc during 1993-1996. Afterwards he has been worked as a senior and principal research scientist in KIST. His main research fields are surface physics, low energy ion beam surface modification, and fundamental research on flexible photoeletronics like LED, photovoltaics/photoelectrochemistry, and photosensor based on II-VI semiconductor, quantum dot, and van der Waals 2D materials.

Yeonjin Yi is a Professor in Department of Physics at Yonsei University. He received his Ph.D. from Yonsei University in Department of Physics in 2005. After working as a postdoctoral researcher at University of South Florida and as a senior researcher at Korea Research Institute of Standard and Science, he joined the Department of Physics, Yonsei University in 2011. His recent research interest is focused on the interfacial electronic structures of molecular solids and low dimensional materials and related devices.

J.Y. Hwang is a Senior Research Scientist of the Institute of Advanced Composite Materials in the Korea Institute of Science and Technology. He has received his BS and MS from Chonbuk National University in South Korea. And he got the Ph. D from University of North Texas at Department of Materials Science and Engineering in 2007. He served as a Research Assistant Professor at Univ. of North Texas before he joined KIST in 2012. His primary research focus is in the area of structure-property-processing relationship in the materials with an emphasis on the hybrid interface using advanced characterization techniques involving TEM and 3-D atom probe tomography.

Dong Ick Son received the ph.D degree in Department of Information Display Engineering from Hanyang University Korea, in 2010. After that, he joined Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) as a star post-doc during 2010-2012. He worked as a visiting researcher in Department of Physics, KU Leuven, Belgium during post-doc period in 2011. Since 2012, he has worked as a senior research scientist in KIST and an Associate Professor at University of Science & Technology (UST). His main research fields are study on optical and electronic devices (nonvolatile organic bistable memory device, light-emitting device, photodetector, and photovoltaic cell) fabricated utilizing hybrid organic/inorganic nanocomposites and study on application devices utilizing hybrid van der Waals 2D materials (including carbon nanomaterials) and inorganic nanoparticles.
